reason: Ukrainian tech job market stats — hardware/embedded/frontend vacancy trends, no AI or startup content
Recruitment tactics for Threads platform — HR topic with no AI or startup content
Military MDMP battle planning with AI agents — interesting AI angle but primary context is military operations, not AI engineering or startups

author_headline: Associate Professor at The Wharton School. Author of Co-Intelligence
post_text:
I had Fable build another thing I always wanted, a full procedural fantasy kingdom generator with economics, trade routes, population growth, wars, lineages, disasters, geography, and occasional dragons. First, I worked with it on a plan, then it made it happen.

You can play it here: https://lnkd.in/eXx83n4t

11 page prompt: https://lnkd.in/epZRiqeD

variants.comment:
The 11-page prompt is the part that caught my attention most — sharing the actual design document alongside the playable result is a rare move.

Curious how Fable handles prompt drift over time: once the world starts generating its own history (wars, lineages, disasters accumulating), does the 11-page prompt stay the authoritative source of truth for what the simulation should be, or does the generated state gradually take over as the real spec? Wondering if you've tried feeding the evolved world state back into a revised prompt, or whether that loop even makes sense in Fable's model.
variants.rationale: The 11-page prompt is the real artifact and raises a genuine open question about whether the prompt or the generated world state becomes the source of truth as simulation history accumulates.

variants.comment:
The "worked on a plan first, then it made it happen" framing is the interesting part here — most people skip straight to prompting for output and skip the planning pass entirely.

What's the failure mode you'd worry about as the simulation runs longer? My read is that the 11-page prompt embeds assumptions about how economies and lineages interact that probably hold for the first few generations but could drift as edge cases compound. Does Fable have a way to surface when the generated world state starts contradicting the design intent baked into the prompt — or is that a feature, not a bug?
variants.rationale: The plan-first then execute workflow is slightly under-examined; pushing on the failure mode when simulation state diverges from prompt assumptions pulls the conversation into sharper territory.

variants.comment:
Sharing the 11-page prompt alongside the playable demo is the right call — the prompt is the actual design artifact, the generated world is just one instantiation of it.

This is worth sitting with: most people will spend 20 minutes playing the kingdom generator and zero minutes reading the prompt. But the prompt is what encodes the economics model, the disaster logic, the lineage rules. The world is disposable; the prompt is what you'd version-control. There's something here about where authorship lives when the output is procedurally generated — the author of the simulation is whoever wrote the 11 pages, not whoever clicked "generate."
variants.rationale: Reframes the post's center of gravity from the playable world to the prompt-as-design-document, arguing the prompt is the persistent artifact and the generated world is disposable.

slack_summary: Used Fable to build a playable procedural fantasy kingdom generator, and shared both the game and the full 11-page prompt behind it.

author_headline: I help teams use AI coding tools without losing control of requirements, tests, architecture and quality | BDD, TDD & Test Automation Expert | Author of BDD in Action
post_text:
Honest question for the BDD crowd: do we still need Gherkin? Spec-driven development needs executable specifications, and Gherkin (Given/When/Then) has been the default way to write them for years.
But I keep wondering whether it's still earning its place.
On the one hand, non-developers can read it, it doubles as living documentation, and the format forces clear thinking about preconditions and outcomes.
But on the other hand: it's a layer on top of your step definitions, and plenty of teams write Gherkin no business stakeholder ever reads. If your acceptance tests are written to be readable anyway, you get the living documentation without the extra layer.
AI complicates things a bit more. AI can turn examples into Gherkin, but it's still pretty rubbish at writing _good_ Gherkin. It's usually capable, though, of turning examples straight into clean, readable acceptance tests. So is the Gherkin step adding understanding, or just translation overhead?
I don't have a settled answer. Where do you land: does Gherkin still pull its weight, or has readable, code-based living documentation made it optional?

variants.comment:
The framing of Gherkin as a readability layer is the right lens — but I'd push back on the conclusion it leads to.

The case against Gherkin usually runs: stakeholders don't read it anyway, so it's just ceremony on top of step definitions. Fair. But I think that argument confuses the artifact with the process. Gherkin's real value was never the file — it was forcing a structured conversation about preconditions and outcomes before anyone touched code. That conversation is what produces the examples. If AI now turns those examples straight into tests, fine, but someone still has to have the conversation that generates the examples in the first place. Gherkin made that mandatory; dropping it doesn't make the conversation happen automatically.

Where I'm genuinely uncertain: does AI change who needs to be in that conversation? If a product manager can now describe an example in plain prose and an AI turns it into a working test, Gherkin starts to look like a format tax paid by engineers for a collaboration problem that could be solved differently. Curious whether your experience with teams using AI coding tools has shifted where you see that conversation breaking down — at the example-gathering stage, or later?
variants.rationale: John leaves room for disagreement; Petro's True BDD thesis (conversation over artifact) gives a genuine counter-position to the readability-layer framing without being hostile.

variants.comment:
The point about AI generating clean, readable acceptance tests directly from examples is the one that keeps nagging at me too.

But here's what I keep coming back to: the bottleneck was never the format. It was the quality of the examples themselves. If a team writes vague examples, AI produces clean tests for vague behavior — and they pass, because the system does exactly what the bad example described. Gherkin's structured syntax at least created friction at the source, forcing people to be specific about "given this exact state" rather than "given the user is logged in somehow." Does readable, code-based living documentation have an equivalent forcing function, or does it just make it easier to be confidently wrong?
variants.rationale: The post debates the format around examples but assumes example quality is a given; this question pulls that blindspot into focus.

variants.strategy_label: Ask a clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The observation that plenty of teams write Gherkin no business stakeholder ever reads is the one that deserves more scrutiny — because it might be diagnosing the wrong problem.

When stakeholders don't read Gherkin, is it because the format is wrong, or because the Gherkin was never actually shown to them — never used as the conversation artifact it was designed to be, and instead written post-hoc by developers as a test-scripting language dressed up in Given/When/Then? In that case, replacing Gherkin with readable code-based tests doesn't solve the underlying collaboration gap; it just removes one more reason to have the conversation. Curious whether the teams you're seeing ditch Gherkin successfully had already solved that collaboration problem by other means, or whether they're just skipping the part where product and engineering align on behavior before writing anything.
variants.rationale: The 'stakeholders don't read it' claim has an unexamined variable — was Gherkin used as a collaboration tool at all? Targets that gap and invites John's field observation.

slack_summary: Asks the BDD community whether Gherkin (Given/When/Then) still earns its place now that AI can turn examples straight into clean, readable acceptance tests.

author_headline: Senior Technology Leader | Technology at the Service of the Business | Experienced in leading and scaling large technology organizations | Startup Advisor | Adjunct lecturer at the University of Geneva
post_text:
AI is making development faster.

So why are many teams not seeing the productivity multiplier they expected?
My current hypothesis: because development (specifications, architecture, coding, testing) was not the only bottleneck. It was just hiding the others.

As AI accelerates local execution, the real constraints become much more visible: dependencies between teams, handoffs between roles, unclear product intent, enterprise platform queues, late validation, and component-based task splitting.

In other words, AI does not automatically improve flow. It accelerates the work until the work hits the organizational wall.

I wrote a longer article on this through the lens of Spec Driven Development and Agentic PDLC : "L'enfer, c'est les autres" : Why AI Exposes Dependency Management as the Real Bottleneck in Software Delivery

Curious where others are seeing the bottleneck move: coding, requirements, dependencies, platform self-service, or organizational design?

variants.comment:
Agreed that coding was hiding the real constraints — the moment the "easy" part gets fast, everything downstream becomes visible.

Where I'd push back slightly: "unclear product intent" isn't just an organizational problem — it's a spec problem that predates the handoff. When the spec isn't the source of truth, intent leaks at every translation step: PM to engineer, engineer to AI, AI to code. You get faster execution of the wrong thing. So in my experience the bottleneck isn't just dependencies between teams, it's that the artifact carrying intent (the spec) degrades before it reaches anyone who could act on it.

Which makes me curious about your Agentic PDLC framing specifically: when you say spec-driven development, do you mean spec-as-source (the spec is the artifact you edit, code gets derived) or spec-as-kickoff (the spec starts the work, then code takes over)? Because those two have very different answers to the dependency problem.
variants.rationale: Sharpens Steve's 'org bottleneck' claim by reframing unclear product intent as a spec-degradation problem upstream of any handoff — directly on Petro's public thesis.

variants.comment:
The "organizational wall" framing is exactly right — AI doesn't improve flow, it makes the ceiling visible faster.

Here's the question that sits in the back of my head though: if agentic PDLC is the proposed fix — autonomous agents spanning the full delivery lifecycle — doesn't that require those same org dependencies to be machine-readable? An agent hitting a handoff between teams still needs to know who to ask, what to ask for, and when the answer is good enough. That's not a tooling problem, it's a governance problem. And governance tends to move at the speed of the slowest stakeholder, not the fastest model. So what does "agentic PDLC" actually mean when the process it's automating is itself political?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation into its sharpest unexamined tension — agentic PDLC solving org bottlenecks requires those bottlenecks to be machine-readable, which they are not.

variants.comment:
The "development was hiding the other bottlenecks" observation is the clearest way I've seen this put.

You list five candidates for where the bottleneck moves: coding, requirements, dependencies, platform self-service, organizational design. Given that you work across both enterprise orgs and startups as an advisor — do you see the bottleneck landing in different places depending on company size? My intuition is that in startups the constraint shifts to requirements clarity almost immediately, while in enterprise it's the dependency/platform queues that dominate. Curious whether that maps to what you're seeing or whether organizational design cuts across both.
variants.rationale: Exploits Steve's dual position as enterprise tech leader and startup advisor to ask a question requiring real comparative experience.

slack_summary: Argues AI speeds up coding but doesn't improve flow — it just exposes the real bottlenecks (team dependencies, handoffs, unclear intent) as the organizational wall.

post_text:
From a thing I'm writing (news soon):

"When code becomes cheaper, you get a lot more of it. However, code that's easier to produce isn't automatically easier to guide to a high-quality outcome. To harness agentic coding at scale, you have to encode your intent so that it's available to coding agents.

That's where spec-driven development (SDD) comes in. A specification or spec is a harness that describes qualities that the solution must have. Spec-driven development is a software engineering approach where a spec is the primary artifact from which the implementation flows. Effective spec-driven development is about channeling intent into the code-generation process, suggesting possibilities, and constraining solutions.

Early adopters of spec-driven development with coding agents focused on unstructured Markdown as a way to build specs, but natural language is too imprecise to reliably specify systems at scale on its own. To effectively capture your expectations, your spec should make use of a variety of formats. Spec-driven development includes any kind of artefact that can guide and constrain code generation — not only Markdown, but contracts, models, examples, and tests."

variants.comment:
The point about intent encoding being the real bottleneck is one I keep running into — more code volume just makes the guidance problem more visible, not more tractable.

One thing your framing leaves open: what validates the spec itself? You describe the spec as the harness that constrains code generation, but in practice I've found that the same LLM that generates the code often generated (or refined) the spec too. When both artifacts come from the same model, passing tests aren't strong evidence that the intent was captured correctly — they're evidence that the model's interpretation of the spec was self-consistent. Curious whether you see a practical answer to that, or whether this is one of the things the piece addresses.
variants.rationale: Opens a precise, unresolved question in Chris's framing (LLM-generated spec validated by LLM-generated tests) without contradicting his thesis.

variants.comment:
The multi-format point lands well — contracts, models, examples, and tests each carry intent that prose can't.

The harder question underneath it: what happens to the spec once the code exists? Most of the tools and practices I've seen treat the spec as the primary artifact at project start, but the code quietly becomes the source of truth the moment an agent (or a human) edits it directly. The spec becomes aspirational documentation rather than a constraint. So the real design challenge might not be "how do we write better specs" but "how do we make editing the code directly more expensive than editing the spec." Does your piece go there?
variants.rationale: Sharpens a blindspot — spec primacy at generation time vs. at iteration time — with a reframe that's contrarian but not hostile.

variants.comment:
Agreed on the Markdown-only path — natural language gives agents too much interpretive freedom, and at scale that freedom accumulates into drift.

One tension I'd push on: the framing here is that the spec is the artifact from which implementation flows, which is true at generation time. But what's the failure mode at iteration time? When a stakeholder asks for a change and the developer edits the code directly — or an agent does — the spec stops being the primary artifact without anyone deciding that. The interesting spec-driven designs I've seen have to answer the question of whether editing the code is even permitted, or whether all changes must come through the spec. That boundary is where "spec-driven" separates into quite different things in practice.
variants.rationale: Introduces the spec-anchored vs. spec-as-source divide as a counter-framing, adding a specific boundary the post doesn't draw.

slack_summary: Previews a piece on spec-driven development: to scale agentic coding you must encode intent as the primary artifact, using not just Markdown but contracts, models, examples, and tests.

post_text:
This repository has never been reviewed by me. It's 100% maintained by robots. It was built from "this is what I need" and "this isn't working as expected. Here's what good looks like." while using my code review and test design review skills.
https://lnkd.in/gB9Mpfqb
Here's the experiment:
Some people claim building things this way will result in  unmaintInable garbage and growing tech debt.
Code review it and demonstrate the claim.

variants.comment:
The challenge is a good one — code review is exactly the right pressure test here, not vibes-based "it feels messy."

The variable I'd add though: the quality of human feedback you're providing isn't free. "Here's what good looks like" presupposes someone who already knows what good looks like — you're essentially running a spec-as-feedback loop with a strong senior reviewer in the chair. The question that would really break or validate the experiment is: what does the maintainability curve look like if the human in the loop is a mid-level engineer who's learned to accept whatever AI produces? Because then you're not testing "can robots maintain a codebase" — you're testing "can a strong human steer robots to maintain a codebase," which is a narrower and more honest claim.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the hidden variable (expert human in the loop) that the experiment conflates with 'robots maintaining code,' pushing Bryan toward a sharper claim boundary.

variants.comment:
The experiment is fair, and I think the people predicting garbage will mostly be wrong — for the wrong reasons.

My counter to the "growing tech debt" crowd isn't about code quality, it's about determinism over time. If two robots build from the same feedback history, do they produce consistent behaviour? Because if the answer is "kind of," then the code is still the source of truth, and someone still has to review everything it produces — which means the human is still the bottleneck. The maintainability question and the consistency question look the same on day one and diverge badly by month six. Curious whether you've seen that show up yet in this repo.
variants.rationale: Brings Petro's thesis (determinism over time is the real test, not day-one code quality) into the debate, adding a concrete dimension beyond what the challengers measure.

variants.comment:
Setting up a public code review as the burden-of-proof mechanism is the right move — claims about unmaintainability tend to evaporate when you have to show the specific line where it breaks down.

One thing I'm curious about: when you say you're using your "code review and test design review skills" as the steering mechanism — how explicit is that feedback? Are you writing it as structured acceptance criteria, or is it more conversational? Asking because the feedback format seems like it could be the actual variable that determines whether this stays coherent as the codebase grows.
variants.rationale: Targets the unexamined input format (structured vs. conversational feedback) as the variable Bryan hasn't addressed.

slack_summary: Shares a repo he's never reviewed, 100% maintained by AI agents, and challenges skeptics to code-review it and prove the 'unmaintainable garbage / tech debt' claim.

author_headline: Deep dives on engineering careers and AI's real impact on developers. Writing The Long Commit on Substack. 20 yrs building software. Sr. Manager, Content @ Auth0.
reason: Generic developer burnout/motivational post with no AI or startup-specific content

post_text:
AI can generate skills in seconds.
The difficult part is knowing whether those changes are actually ready to merge.
Tessl introduces the new Tessl Review, a major upgrade that helps teams review AI-generated skill changes against the standards that matter to their organisation.
Every team has a different idea of what "good" looks like. Some care about architecture patterns, others prioritise security, coding conventions, domain-specific requirements, or internal best practices. A single default score can't capture all of that.
With custom review rubrics, you can define your own review criteria and evaluate skills against your team's standards, not somebody else's.
The new review experience also brings more accurate agentic reviews, a shared history of review runs, and tessl review fix to help improve skills that don't meet the quality bar.
Because faster software delivery shouldn't come at the cost of quality.
Read the blog to learn how Tessl Review helps teams build, review, and improve skills with confidence.
https://lnkd.in/gnxgtHny

variants.comment:
The rubrics point is the real unlock here — a "good" score meaning something different for a fintech team vs a data pipeline team vs an OSS lib is exactly the right problem to name.

Curious how the rubric evaluation works under the hood: does Tessl Review run the rubric as an LLM judge against the skill diff, or is there something more deterministic involved? The failure mode I'd worry about is rubric drift — the LLM grades the same change differently on Monday vs Friday, which means your quality bar shifts without anyone noticing. Has that come up in testing?
variants.rationale: Tessl's post leaves the rubric evaluation mechanism unaddressed — an LLM-judge implementation has a well-known non-determinism problem that directly undercuts the quality-gate promise, making this the sharpest specific question to ask.

variants.comment:
Custom review rubrics fix a real thing — org-specific standards can't be encoded in a generic score, and teams waste time arguing about whether AI output meets "their" bar rather than a shared one.

The thing I keep turning over: if the spec is the source of truth, shouldn't the spec itself be the rubric? The review question then becomes "does the generated skill match the spec" — deterministic, not LLM-judged. Custom rubrics are a good answer for the world where code stays the source of truth. But if that assumption ever shifts, the rubric layer dissolves. Is the Tessl bet that code stays the source of truth indefinitely, or is the rubric a bridge while model reliability catches up?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation into Petro's core thesis territory (spec as source of truth) without pitching TrueBDD by name — frames it as a genuine strategic question about Tessl's long-term architecture bet.

variants.comment:
The observation that faster delivery shouldn't come at the cost of quality is one worth defending — most "ship fast, fix later" arguments quietly assume the cost of "later" is manageable, which is usually wrong.

Where I'd push back a little: custom rubrics still assume someone needs to evaluate the generated code against those rubrics. That keeps review on the critical path — the rubric makes it smarter but not necessarily faster. The alternative bet is to move the source of truth to the spec and reduce the review surface rather than improve it: if the spec is what's reviewed and the code is derived from it, you're not grading the output, you're grading the input. Both are real bets. Curious whether Tessl sees rubrics as the long-term answer or a bridge while underlying model reliability closes the gap.
variants.rationale: Engages Tessl's implicit assumption (code stays the source of truth, therefore review stays on the critical path) from Petro's documented contrarian position, without dismissing their work or naming TrueBDD as a competitor.

slack_summary: Tessl launches "Tessl Review" — custom rubrics that let teams grade AI-generated code changes against their own org-specific standards instead of one generic score.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

variants.comment:
The "gravity well of discarded ideas" is the most precise framing of this problem I've seen — stale comments aren't just noise, they're literally attractor states that pull the next generation toward decisions that were already rejected.

Curious what you've landed on as the actual remedy. Is it a deletion discipline (if a comment describes what code used to do, it gets cut immediately), or are you experimenting with a structured format that makes the model-facing intent explicit rather than archaeological?
variants.rationale: Jonathan identified a sharp mechanism; asking what fix he's found in practice extends his thought without flattering.

variants.comment:
The "better the model, the more convoluted the code" observation is real and I think under-examined. The model doesn't just accept the context you give it — it tries to be consistent with it, so a codebase full of half-baked iterations becomes a very persuasive prior.

The part I'd push on: is pruning comments in the IDE the right intervention, or is it a symptom that we're still treating code as the ground truth the model reasons from? If the spec were the source of truth and code were genuinely disposable — regenerated from spec rather than incrementally patched — the comment archaeology problem mostly disappears. The question is whether the "slow is smooth" answer is "better IDE hygiene" or "wrong layer altogether."
variants.rationale: Pushes on whether IDE pruning solves the root cause or patches a symptom, opening the spec-as-source-of-truth angle without pitching TrueBDD.

variants.comment:
The boomerang back to the IDE makes sense — at some point you need eyes on what's actually in the file, not just what the model tells you is there.

Where I'd push back slightly: the "cut verbose comments" workflow feels like a patch on a deeper issue. Code is being used as the model's memory — and commented-out iteration history is just the most visible symptom. The underlying problem is that the model's context is the code, so everything that accumulates in the code (including ghost comments) becomes its worldview. I'm skeptical the solution scales past "discipline." At some point the pressure is to stop treating code as the source of truth at all — let the spec carry that weight and treat the code as something you can delete and regenerate. Whether that's practical today is a different question, but the trend Jonathan describes seems to push in that direction whether we intend it to or not.
variants.rationale: Frames Petro's core thesis (code is disposable, spec is source of truth) as a genuine counter-position to comment hygiene as the fix, without naming TrueBDD.

slack_summary: Reflects that he's back to looking at the IDE more, mainly to cut AI-generated stale comments that record what code used to do and pull the model back toward discarded ideas.
